author: brunowinter2000
text:
=== SESSION 2026-03-03: Architektur-Pivot — Custom Scraper → Crawl4AI ===

ENTSCHEIDUNG: Profil-basiertes Scraper-System komplett ersetzen durch Crawl4AI (61k Stars, Apache 2.0).

BEGRÜNDUNG:
- Profil-System skaliert nicht: jede Website braucht manuelles CSS-Klassen-Mapping
- Debug-Loop (Fix→Test→Commit) erfordert zu viel Babysitting
- Jeder Fix riskiert Regressionen auf anderen Domains
- Die 3 offenen Wiki-Bugs (Broken Images, Absatzumbrüche, leere Headings) werden obsolet

CRAWL4AI VORTEILE:
- fit_markdown: heuristisch gefilterter Output, ersetzt ganzes Profil-System
- Playwright built-in: JS-Rendering ohne eigene Browser-Verwaltung
- LLM-Extraction: Schema-basierte Datenextraktion (für später)
- PruningContentFilter + BM25ContentFilter
- 50k+ Community, battle-tested

ARCHITEKTUR:
- Crawl4AI als Python Library in bestehendem MCP Server (nicht Docker)
- server.py bleibt MCP-Orchestrator (FastMCP)
- Tool-Signaturen bleiben gleich (scrape_url, search_web) — keine Breaking Changes
- Entfernt werden: content_filter.py, html_parser.py, markdown_converter.py, routing.py, profiles.yml
- scrape_url.py wird auf Crawl4AI umgebaut
- src/searxng/ bleibt unverändert

FOKUS-SHIFT: Von Scraping zu Search. MCP Server ist searxng — Scraping ist nur Mittel zum Zweck.

author: Bruno Winter
text:
SESSION 2026-03-10: Tor-Proxy implementiert und getestet.

IMPLEMENTIERUNG:
- docker-compose.yml: peterdavehello/tor-socks-proxy Container hinzugefuegt (630 Stars, 10MB Alpine, SOCKS5 auf Port 9150)
- settings.yml: outgoing.proxies = socks5h://tor:9150, using_tor_proxy = true, extra_proxy_timeout = 10
- extra_proxy_timeout musste int sein (nicht float) — SearXNG Schema-Validierung schlug fehl mit 10.0
- Platform-Warnung: Image ist linux/amd64, Host ist arm64 (Apple Silicon) — laeuft via Rosetta

TESTERGEBNISSE TOR:
- Brave: FUNKTIONIERT ueber Tor (stabil, liefert Ergebnisse)
- Startpage: FUNKTIONIERT ueber Tor (= Google-Ergebnisse via Proxy)
- Google: HTTP 400 Bad Request ueber Tor — Google blockt Tor-Exit-Nodes komplett, haerter als CAPTCHA
- DDG: HTTP 403 Access Denied ueber Tor — Tor-Exit-Nodes werden erkannt und geblockt
- Google Scholar: nicht getestet

TESTERGEBNISSE DDG OHNE TOR (using_tor_proxy: false per Engine):
- DDG ueber Eduroam-WLAN (Bibliothek): 403 Access Denied
- DDG ueber Handy-Hotspot (andere IP): 403 Access Denied
- DDG via curl mit Browser User-Agent direkt vom Mac: HTTP 202 OK
- Fazit: Nicht die IP ist das Problem, sondern SearXNG's Request-Pattern wird als Bot erkannt (TLS Fingerprint, Sec-Fetch Headers, VQD Token Handling)

RECHERCHE GOOGLE:
- Reddit r/Searx (1o0p4gf, 5 Monate alt): Kein Workaround, nur Docker-Image-Update hilft temporaer
- Reddit r/selfhosted (1rea4hc, 13 Tage alt): Community empfiehlt Startpage statt Google in SearXNG
- SearXNG Docs admin_answer-captcha.md: SSH-Tunnel + Browser-CAPTCHA-Loesung — nur fuer remote Server relevant, nicht fuer lokales Docker
- Kein bekannter Workaround fuer Google ueber Tor auf Reddit oder GitHub

RECHERCHE DDG:
- GitHub PR #5758 (MERGED 2026-02-22): Kompletter DDG-Engine-Overhaul mit VQD Token Harvesting — ist in unserem Image (02.03.2026) enthalten, loest das Problem aber nicht
- GitHub PR #5839 (OPEN 2026-03-10): Rate Limiter + CAPTCHA Cooldown — noch nicht gemerged
- GitHub Issue #4824 (OPEN): 28 Kommentare, Root-Cause-Analyse von @return42 — DDG nutzt stateless Bot-Klassifikation (TLS Fingerprint + VQD Token Lifecycle)
- SearXNG Docs dev_engines_online_duckduckgo.md: DDG blockt auf IP-Ebene, sliding window (1h ohne Requests = Unblock), Browser-Query kann IP entsperren — hat bei uns nicht funktioniert
- Docs bestaetigen: DDG has no client session, IP blocking is stateless
- Reddit: Keine DDG-spezifischen Workarounds gefunden

FAZIT:
- Google und DDG sind fuer Self-Hosted SearXNG aktuell unzuverlaessig
- Google: Tor macht es schlimmer (400 statt CAPTCHA), direkt = intermittent CAPTCHAs
- DDG: Bot-Erkennung auf SearXNG-Ebene, unabhaengig von IP oder Proxy
- Stabile Engines: Startpage (Google-Proxy) + Brave ueber Tor
- Potenzielle zusaetzliche Engines: Ecosia, Qwant (eigener Index, nicht getestet)

author: Bruno Winter
text:
ROOT CAUSE GEFUNDEN UND GELOEST:

PROBLEM: using_tor_proxy: false per Engine reicht NICHT aus um Tor zu umgehen. Die proxies-Config wird trotzdem vom globalen Default geerbt. DDG/Google gingen weiterhin ueber socks5h://tor:9150 und wurden dort geblockt.

BEWEIS: 
- docker exec searxng python: DDG Network using_tor_proxy=False ABER proxies={'all://': ['socks5h://tor:9150']}
- httpx.post() direkt im Container OHNE Proxy: HTTP 200
- SearXNG Engine Request DURCH Proxy: HTTP 403

LOESUNG: proxies: {} explizit per Engine setzen:
  - name: duckduckgo
    using_tor_proxy: false
    proxies: {}
  - name: google  
    using_tor_proxy: false
    proxies: {}

ERGEBNIS: Alle 4 Engines funktionieren:
- Brave: ueber Tor (20 Ergebnisse)
- Startpage: ueber Tor (10 Ergebnisse, = Google-Proxy)
- Google: direkt ohne Proxy (10 Ergebnisse)
- DDG: direkt ohne Proxy (10 Ergebnisse)
- Qwant: blockt auch direkt, disabled

ARCHITEKTUR: Tor fuer Brave+Startpage (IP-Rotation noetig), direkt fuer Google+DDG (Tor-Exit-Nodes werden geblockt, aber direkte IP funktioniert mit SearXNG's Bot-Mitigation PR #5758)

ERKENNTNISSE:

- remove_overlay_elements=True entfernt (redundant mit excluded_selector fuer Cookie-Banner)
- [class*='cky-'] → [class*='cky-consent'], [class*='cky-banner'] (spezifischer fuer CookieYes)
- Scrape-Reihenfolge umgedreht: Normal-Browser zuerst, Stealth nur als Fallback bei leerem Content
- MIN_CONTENT_THRESHOLD=200 Konstante: fit_markdown < 200 chars → raw_markdown Fallback

Sachverhalt: Google now shows 'Before you continue / We use cookies and data' INLINE on search URL (not redirect to consent.google.com). Sachverhalt: The 2026-04-07 '30/30 baseline' in decisions/stealth01_detection_layers.md was measured against dev/search_pipeline/engines_eval/_stress_browser.py, NOT against production src/search/browser.py. Sachverhalt: Beschreibt Tor-Routing über `src/searxng/settings.yml` (beides seit Engine-Cut 2026-04-15 weg). Vollständig stale, kein Bezug mehr zum aktiven Code. Nicht kritisch, aber Altlast.

author: Bruno Winter
text:
STAND 2026-04-22 (stress-break + research):

DONE (cross-session, short):
- Baseline 30/30 Google Smoke stack stabil (pydoll headless, SOCS per-tab, `#rso h3`+`.MjjYud`, 0 delay)
- Stress-Test Batch 1: 30/30 → 27/30 → 28/30 → 0/30 — harter IP-Block nach ~90 kumulativen Queries / ~10 min
- Break-Attribution: IP-Level Layer 5, NICHT Fingerprint (Run 4 Nav 537ms stabil + DOM 0ms + instant `/sorry/` ab Q1)
- `/sorry/` empirisch identifiziert als reCAPTCHA Enterprise v2 Checkbox, dt. Locale, sitekey `6LfwuyUTAAAAAOAmoS0fdqijC2PbbdH4kjq62Y1b`
- `decisions/stealth05_rate_limiting.md` Evidenz + SOLL-Section updated, `stealth00` Pointer ergänzt
- `dev/search_pipeline/_capture_sorry.py` (229 LOC, standalone) Helper für Block-Captures; Artefakte (PNG/HTML/MD) gitignored (enthalten öffentliche IP)
- Experiment-Log in DOCS um 4 stress-run-Zeilen ergänzt
- `sources/sources.md` + 3 Einträge: yagooglesearch, karust/openserp, 2captcha/2captcha-go
- GH-Research Layer-5-Mitigation abgeschlossen: Community-Landschaft klar, siehe OPEN

OPEN (detailliert):

### config.yml Delay-Change (Critical, trivial)
Sachverhalt: `dev/search_pipeline/config.yml` hat aktuell `delay_between_queries: 0`. Empirie zeigt das bricht bei ~90 kumulativen Queries. `karust/openserp` (aktives Repo, Commits April 2026) defaultet Google auf `rate_requests: 4, rate_burst: 2` = ~15s/query. Das ist weit unter jeder plausiblen Schwelle, belässt Burst-Toleranz für 1-2 schnelle Queries am Anfang.
Implementierung: `delay_between_queries: 0 → uniform(12, 18)` in config.yml. ABER: `01_google_smoke.py` Zeile 47 nutzt `asyncio.sleep(cfg["run"]["delay_between_queries"])` — muss zu random-draw werden (z.B. `random.uniform(cfg["run"]["delay_between_queries_min"], cfg["run"]["delay_between_queries_max"])` oder einfacher Float mit Jitter-Funktion). Analog `src/search/rate_limiter.py` Token-Bucket auf 4 req/min + burst 2 setzen (prüfen ob aktueller Wert anders).
Next step: Worker-Task, eine Config + zwei Code-Stellen. 10 Minuten Arbeit.

### Agent-Skill 4-Query-Pattern (Important, larger refactor)
Sachverhalt: `skills/agent-web-research/SKILL.md` + `agents/web-research.md` beschreiben aktuell "5+ queries, pagination pageno 1-3, general+science". Mit 4 Engines × 5+ Queries × 3 Pages = bis zu 60 Requests pro Agent-Run. Bei gedrosselter 4 req/min dauert das pro Engine 15 Minuten. Passt nicht zu "agentic search = schnell viele Ergebnisse".
Zielarchitektur laut Session-Entscheid: 4 Queries pro Engine × N Engines (Google + Bing + Scholar + CrossRef = 4 aktive + evtl. ArXiv/GitHub/Reddit via Plugin-Routing = effektiv 4-7) parallel. Dedup über Engines ergibt ~80 URLs. "Wir gehen nicht full Google" — Coverage kommt durch Engine-Diversität, nicht durch Google-Volume.
Next step: User entscheidet ob zuerst config.yml-change (quick) oder direkt agent-refactor (bigger). Beide können parallel in zwei Workern laufen — keine File-Overlap (config.yml vs skills/agents/). Für agent-refactor: lesen des aktuellen Skills + Agent-File, Umbau auf 4-Queries-Target, Engine-List-Check ob aktuelle Routing-Logik das unterstützt.

### Tor empirisch testen (Important, 15min task)
Sachverhalt: yagooglesearch-README (2024) sagt "Tor will likely get you blocked quickly". Das ist 2-Jahre-alte Community-Hearsay — wir haben's nie selbst getestet. Aus Library-WLAN-Kontext gibt es zusätzliche Unsicherheit: Library-DPI könnte SOCKS5 auf Port 9050 blocken. Beide Fragen in einem Run beantwortbar.
Protokoll: Tor lokal laufen lassen (`brew install tor && tor &`), Browser-Config auf `socks5h://127.0.0.1:9050` setzen (pydoll unterstützt proxy-Flag via ChromiumOptions), 30-Query-Smoke fahren. Drei mögliche Ergebnisse: (a) Tor funktioniert → 30/30, wir haben kostenlose IP-Rotation; (b) Google blockt Tor-Exits → `/sorry/` ab Q1, Community-Hearsay bestätigt; (c) Library-DPI blockt SOCKS5 → Connection refused, wir wissen Library-Firewall verhindert diese Option.
Next step: Als einzelner Test in neuer Session, vor oder nach config.yml-change egal. Opus führt direkt aus (dev script run — oder Worker wenn Source-Edit am Smoke-Script nötig wird für Proxy-Flag).

### Proxy-Pool-Architektur (conditional next-tier)
Sachverhalt: Nur relevant falls Tor broken UND Agentic-Search-Volume-Bedarf sich als >80 Queries/Session rausstellt. Aktuell nicht etabliert. Kommerzielle Pools (Bright Data/Oxylabs) fallen raus per User-Ansage "zahlen wir auf keinen Fall". Residuale Optionen: Cloud-Free-Tier IPs (Oracle Always-Free 2 VMs, AWS Free-Tier 12 Monate). Google flagged Datacenter-IPs tendenziell aggressiver als Residential — Setup-Effort mittel, Ertrag unklar.
Architektur: `src/search/browser.py` per-request Proxy-Injection via `ChromiumOptions.add_argument(--proxy-server=...)`, Proxy-Health-Checking (openserp Pattern: `failure_threshold: 3 → disable`), Retry-mit-anderer-IP-Logik. Ist bereits in `src/search/rate_limiter.py` Token-Bucket-Pattern angedacht, aber per-IP-tracking fehlt.
Next step: NICHT implementieren bis (a) Tor-Test negativ und (b) Volume-Bedarf >80 Queries/Session konkret ist. Bis dahin ist defensive Delay + 4-Query-Agent-Pattern die komplette Lösung.

APPROACH: Diese Session = 2 Worker-Runden (stress-test + stress-test follow-up mit IP-Sanitization). Beide konvergent geplant, straight-forward gemerged. GH-Research als Opus-direkt (nicht Worker per Rule). Next session: 2 parallele Worker möglich (config.yml-change + agent-skill-refactor → keine File-Overlap), plus Tor-Test als dritter Einzel-Run. Worker dieser Session wurde am Ende gekillt — Fresh-Start.

Architektur-Vision: 4 Tabs reserviert pro Engine (engine-übergreifender Browser-Pool von max 4-7 Engines × 4 Tabs = 16-28 Tabs total in 4GB RAM-Cap), Google's empirisches Rate-Limit (~4 reqs/min) als Baseline für alle (auch wenn andere Engines mehr könnten — Uniformität).

Hypothese: mit --cooldown 30 oder --cooldown 60 zwischen den 4er-Bursts wären's 30/30 weil Google-Rate-Limit-Window weniger angekratzt wird. Triviales Re-Run mit dem existierenden 02_burst_smoke.py (--cooldown 60 --max-queries 30, ~9 min wall time).
Next step: Eigenständiger Run beliebig zeitlich (kein Worker nötig — Opus oder User kann direkt feuern). Ergebnis als zusätzlicher Datenpunkt für Architektur-Validation.

Sachverhalt: derive_status in 02_burst_smoke.py nutzt aktuell count-precedence-workaround weil stderr im Batch-Subprocess shared ist. Saubere Lösung wäre cli.py's search_batch zu erweitern: pro Query einen status-Header in stdout schreiben (z.B. "## Query N: <q> [status=OK]" vor jedem Result-Block), dann parsed der Smoke pro-Query exakt statt indirekt über count.
Next step: ~10 LOC Änderung in cli.py + parse logic in 02_burst_smoke.py. Worker-Task wenn jemand das anfasst, low priority — aktueller workaround funktioniert.

Sachverhalt: yagooglesearch-README sagt Tor wird wahrscheinlich geblockt. Library-WLAN-Kontext könnte zusätzlich SOCKS5 auf Port 9050 blocken. Beides in einem Test beantwortbar: brew install tor && tor &, Browser-Config auf socks5h://127.0.0.1:9050, 30-Query-Smoke fahren.

author: Bruno Winter
text:
## Step 3 (dual-mode hands-on) — key findings on Q24 (trafilatura vs readability)

scrape-prep worker delivered the dual-mode comparison report at `dev/scrape_pipeline/01_dual_mode_outputs/20260505_185247/01_dual_mode_report.md` (commit 10a3648 on branch scrape-prep). 20 URLs from Q24, both Mode 1 (scrape_url_raw) and Mode 2 (scrape_url) in parallel via asyncio. 67s total runtime.

Counts: Mode 1 16/20 success, Mode 2 18/20 success — but two of those 18 are PDFs returning ~90 bytes whitespace that aren't caught by garbage detection. True clean success rate ≈ 16/20 for both modes, with 4 hard structural failures (2 plugin-routed: github + arxiv; 2 PDFs).

### Five key findings for the Mode 1 / Mode 2 design discussion

**1. Compression ratio is extreme (16:1 in places).** seirdy.one/website-best-practices: M1 raw = 228 KB, M2 filtered = 14 KB. HN thread (51K → 15K) hits the cap. The PruningFilter@0.48 strips aggressively. Open question: how much of what it strips is signal vs boilerplate? Need per-URL diff or sample reading to judge.

**2. Mode 2's 15K char cap hits 8 of 18 successful runs.** For Mode 2 (lean for relevance) the cap is plausibly OK. For Mode 1 (RAG indexing), no cap exists by design — seirdy-style pages deliver 228K and 148K. Mode 1's pipeline must either decouple from the 15K cap or treat the cap as configurable per-mode.

**3. Boilerplate chrome leaks in both modes — confirms the cleanup-script hypothesis.**
- Mode 2 (PruningFilter): readthedocs.io previews start with `Skip to main content / Back to top / Light Dark System Settings / GitHub link / ad block`. HN's table layout comes through as raw markdown table chrome — filter doesn't recognize it.
- Mode 1 (raw): chuniversiteit.nl starts with logo image link and nav bar. Expected for raw markdown.
- This is direct evidence for why `dev/cleanup/clean_web_*.py` exists — domain-specific patches for chrome the filter doesn't strip. Library replacement (trafilatura, jusText) is now justified by concrete failures, not just speculation.

**4. PDF gap in both modes.** #3 (bevendorff_2023b.pdf) and #14 (Thesis_Template_UDE.pdf):
- Mode 1: returns `no_content` — Crawl4AI extracts nothing from PDF bytes.
- Mode 2: returns 87/100 bytes whitespace, NOT caught by garbage detection. The `if not content:` check passes because a whitespace-only string is truthy in Python. False-positive `ok`.
- Architectural gap: the pipeline needs explicit PDF-URL detection BEFORE scrape (e.g. .pdf extension or HEAD-fetch content-type=application/pdf) and route to `download_pdf` instead. Existing `download_pdf` CLI is the consumer, but no routing happens at scrape-entry today. This is also relevant to bead searxng-pfk (Paper Mode) — it's the same architectural gap surfacing.

**5. doi.org redirects work cleanly.** #13/#15/#16/#20 — doi.org redirects to aclanthology.org / dl.acm.org / qeios.com and the destination scrapes cleanly in both modes. Important data point: the academic-class URLs from search are USABLE in scrape, not just discovery surface.

### Implications for next steps

- GH library research (Step 2 originally skipped) is now justified. Findings 3 (boilerplate) + 1 (compression-vs-signal question) need data we don't have without trying alternatives. Trafilatura specifically advertises better boilerplate handling on docs sites; jusText is built for boilerplate removal.
- PDF detection (finding 4) is a small architectural addition independent of the library question. Belongs to bead searxng-pfk (Paper Mode) or its own follow-up.
- 15K cap question (finding 2) is a Mode 1 design parameter — should be set per-mode in the redesign.
- The dev script `01_dual_mode_smoke.py` is a reproducible eval tool — we can re-run on different libraries / different filter settings to compare before committing to a library choice.

author: Bruno Winter
text:
## Mode 2 quality drill-down — per-URL evidence on Q24 outputs

After merging the dual-mode report, I read 4 representative Mode 2 outputs (URLs #2 chuniversiteit, #4 trafilatura-readthedocs, #10 HN Defuddle thread, #17 seirdy.one). Four concrete finding classes ranked by severity:

### 1. Crawl4AI progress noise leaks into ALL M2 outputs (wrapper bug)

Every M2 file starts with 6 lines of Crawl4AI telemetry:

```
[FETCH]... ↓ <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 1.67s
[SCRAPE].. ◆ <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 0.14s
[COMPLETE] ● <url>
| ✓ | ⏱: 1.82s
```

Not page content — Crawl4AI's stderr/stdout telemetry leaking through our wrapper. Approximately 108 lines (6 × 18 successful URLs) of pure noise. Independent of any library swap. Easy fix in `src/scraper/scrape_url.py`: regex-strip these patterns before returning to user. Pure win, no trade-offs. Suspect this affects M1 too — needs spot-check.

### 2. Sphinx/Furo doc-site nav chrome leaks (selector gap)

URL #4 trafilatura.readthedocs.io/evaluation.html — first lines after Crawl4AI noise:

```
[Skip to main content](...)
Back to top
Light Dark System Settings
[ GitHub](...)
[**Enterprise docs hosting** ...]   ← inline ad block
[ℹ️](...)
```

PruningFilter@0.48 doesn't strip Sphinx/Furo theme chrome. Six lines of nav + one inline ad block before the actual "# Evaluation" heading. Lösungspfad: extend `COOKIE_CONSENT_SELECTOR` in `src/scraper/scrape_url.py` (DOM-level exclude before Crawl4AI's pruning) with Sphinx/Furo patterns: `[role="navigation"]`, `.theme-toggle`, `.ethical-ad`, `[id^="ethical-ad"]`. DOM-level is robuster than markdown-level filtering. Independent of library swap.

### 3. Silent content corruption in tables — DANGEROUS

URL #2 chuniversiteit.nl comparison table. Source table has 3 columns: Extractor (with external links to each extractor) | Language | Approach. After Mode 2 filtering:

```
| Extractor  | Language  | Approach  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Python  | Heuristic: HTML tag distribution  |    ← Extractor name MISSING
| Python  | Heuristic: rule-based  |              ← MISSING
| [Trafilatura](...)  | Python  | Heuristic: rule-based  |   ← present (self-link)
```

The Extractor column was dropped on rows where the cell contained an EXTERNAL link (Readability, Goose3, etc.). Only Trafilatura survived because it's a self-reference internal link. Result: table looks normal but the key dimension (which extractor) is gone. PruningFilter@0.48 treats external links as boilerplate and removes them, dabei sind sie hier essenzielle Daten.

This is the worst class of issue: no garbage detection fires, no status error, output looks OK, but signal is silently deleted. Cannot be fixed by selector tweaks — this is a fundamental misclassification by the filter logic. **This is the trigger for the library-swap question.** Trafilatura's stated strength is exactly tables and link-content classification (Bevendorff 2023 benchmark winner). If Trafilatura extracts this table correctly, library swap is justified empirically.

### 4. HN threads — content preserved but verbose markdown table chrome

URL #10 HN Defuddle thread. Each comment formatted as markdown table cell with `| --- |` separators. Estimated 30-40% of bytes are table syntax. Content is THERE and readable, just inefficient. Filter doesn't recognize HN's layout as navigation. Niche fix — domain-specific cleanup pass after generic extraction would be the path. Not a library question.

### 5. Clean cases (control)

URL #17 seirdy.one website-best-practices: text-heavy article, ~14 KB Mode 2 output, headers preserved, code blocks intact, no chrome. PruningFilter@0.48 works well on text-dominant content.
URL #2 chuniversiteit.nl text portions: clean prose, only the comparison table is corrupted (point 3).
doi.org redirects (#13/#15/#16/#20): all resolve cleanly, downstream content scrapes well.

### Implications for design strategy

- **Findings 1 + 2 are independent of library choice** — fix in current Crawl4AI wrapper, immediate value.
- **Finding 3 is the library-swap trigger.** Concrete A/B test: re-run Q24 URLs through trafilatura with same side-by-side format. If trafilatura extracts the chuniversiteit table correctly AND removes Sphinx chrome on trafilatura-readthedocs, the library swap is empirically justified. If not, stay on Crawl4AI + selector extensions.
- **Finding 4** — domain-specific post-cleanup pass, low priority, address after main library decision.

### Reproducible eval tool

`dev/scrape_pipeline/01_dual_mode_smoke.py` is the reusable A/B harness. Re-run on the same Q24 URLs against any library by replacing the cli.py-subprocess invocation. The 18 baseline outputs in `mode2_filtered/` are the reference for comparison.

- 4 finding classes captured in earlier bead comments
- Step 2 (GH library research) deferred per user direction — only revisit on specific failure evidence

OPEN (detailed):

### Library A/B test: Trafilatura vs Crawl4AI+PruningFilter@0.48
Sachverhalt: Finding 3 from Mode-2 drill-down (silent table-content corruption on chuniversiteit.nl — external-link cells dropped) is an architecture-level fault that selector tweaks cannot fix. The PruningFilter classifies external links as boilerplate and removes table data with them. Trafilatura's stated strength (Bevendorff 2023 winner) is exactly content-vs-boilerplate classification including links inside content.
Concrete A/B: re-run 01_dual_mode_smoke.py on the same Q24 URLs against Trafilatura (replace cli.py-scrape_url subprocess with a Trafilatura wrapper). Compare side-by-side against the 18 baseline outputs in dev/scrape_pipeline/01_dual_mode_outputs/20260505_185247/mode2_filtered/.
Decision criteria: if Trafilatura extracts the chuniversiteit comparison table correctly (Extractor column intact) AND removes Sphinx/Furo chrome on trafilatura.readthedocs.io, library swap is empirically justified. If not, stay on Crawl4AI + extend COOKIE_CONSENT_SELECTOR for Sphinx patterns.
Next step: spawn worker to implement Trafilatura A/B harness (likely a small extension to 01_dual_mode_smoke.py with a --library flag). Then user+Opus review side-by-side. Then design Mode 1 / Mode 2 architecture (filter cap per-mode; dev/scrape_pipeline/mode_eval/ scaffolding; etc).

### Finding 4 follow-up — HN/forum markdown overhead
Niche, low priority. Domain-specific cleanup pass after generic extraction (HN tables produce 30-40% markdown table syntax overhead but content is preserved). Defer until Mode 1 / Mode 2 architecture is settled.

### Crawl4AI noise (Finding 1) and Sphinx chrome (Finding 2)
Spawned as searxng-5h5 (noise) and tracked here for the selector-extension piece. Both wrapper/selector fixes, independent of library swap, can run in parallel to A/B.

APPROACH: scrape-prep worker built reusable A/B harness (01_dual_mode_smoke.py) that parses search-results MD format and runs both production CLI modes in parallel via asyncio. Architecture is library-swappable — replacing the scrape_url subprocess with another extractor reuses everything else (parsing, output structure, comparison report generation).

STAND 2026-05-05:

DONE (short):
- Bead created with full scope: PDF discovery + batch download + search_web --paper-mode flag, architectural relationship to scrape redesign documented

OPEN (detailed):

### Wait for searxng-oft (scrape redesign) to settle
Sachverhalt: Paper Mode sits on top of the scrape pipeline. Library choice (Crawl4AI vs Trafilatura vs hybrid) determines how PDF link extraction is implemented. Mode 1 vs Mode 2 architecture determines where PDF detection plugs in (before scrape, as routing rule).
Next step: after oft closes (Mode 1/Mode 2 architecture finalized + library decided), design Paper Mode flow:
1. Direct PDF URL → already handled by cli.py download_pdf (no change)
2. Page with PDF hyperlinks → new component: extract <a href="*.pdf"> from scraped content, optionally HEAD-fetch content-type=application/pdf for non-extension PDFs
3. Search-side --paper-mode flag → ranking signal toward known-PDF-bearing domains (arXiv direct PDFs, Springer Open Access, doi.org with PDF preview)

### Concrete trigger
Finding 4 from oft Step 3 drill-down: PDF gap in current pipeline (M1 returns no_content, M2 returns whitespace as false 'ok'). When Mode 1 / Mode 2 redesign happens, PDF detection MUST be addressed in the same architectural pass — this is the natural anchor.
